A strong and powerful storm brought heavy snows to central Montana during the Christmas holiday season, December 26 and 27. A swath of snow (over 3 feet of snow fall) fell from northern Meagher to Southern Phillips counties. The storm tied the maximum 24-hour snowfall total for Montana when 48 inches fell at Millegan 14SE (near the Cascade-Meagher county line). Reported snow fall amounts include: 53 inches at Shonkin 7S, 48 inches near Millegan 14SE, 41 inches at the Crystal Lake SNOTEL site, 36 inches at Hobson, 25 inches at the Onion Park SNOTEL site, 24 inches at Stanford, 20 inches at Lewistown, 14 inches at Chinook, 13 inches at Valentine, 12 inches at Grassrange, 12 inches at Utica, 12 inches at Bozeman, and 5 inches at Ennis and White Sulphur Springs. The heavy snow was notable for its high water content. Water equivalents of 2 to 3 inches were common with the storm.  In addition to the heavy snow, northwest wind gusts up to 50 mph created blizzard conditions over portions of central and eastern Montana. Snow drifts 3 to 4 feet high were reported in some areas.The storm caused dozens of traffic accidents including numerous semi tractor-trailers sliding off roads or into ditches. Hundreds of motorists were stranded and many roads were closed including portions of Interstate 90. Holiday air travelers were also stranded as airlines canceled many flights. Some ski areas were forced to postpone opening due to the heavy snow. Bridger Bowl (a ski area 16 miles north of Bozeman) received more than 5 feet of snow and could not open as planned on the 26th.

A powerful winter storm affected Southern Montana and Northern Wyoming during a three day period from December 26, 2003 through December 28, 2003.  A strong arctic front ushered in colder air across the area as a deep trough moved across Montana. A moist southwest flow aloft moved over this cold airmass at the surface and produced a prolonged period of snow across the area.  In addition...a deep surface low moved across Wyoming into South Dakota resulting in strong northerly winds of 25 to 40 mph which produced significant blowing and drifting snow. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front crossed northeast Montana during the morning hours of the 17th.  Strong gradient winds occurred immediately behind the front.  Sustained wind speeds of 40 to 50 mph were common with wind gusts of up to 68 mph.  The highest measured wind gust was 68 mph at King Coulee Raws in northern Garfield county.  A semi truck and trailer was blown off Interstate 94 in Dawson County, and overturned while attempting to exit the highway.  The driver suffered minor injuries.

A strong cold front crossed northeast Montana during the morning hours of the 17th.  Strong gradient winds occurred immediately behind the front.  Sustained wind speeds of 40 to 50 mph were common with wind gusts of up to 68 mph.  The highest measured wind gust was 68 mph at King Coulee Raws in northern Garfield county.  A semi truck and trailer was blown off Interstate 94 in Dawson County, and overturned while attempting to exit the highway.  The driver suffered minor injuries.

The Missouri Breaks Complex Fires started on July 16th due to lightning strikes.  The complex of fires included four fires in Eastern Garfield County.  The four fires included the Germain, Ghost Coulee, Big Coulee and Indian fires and was classified as a type two incident.  The complex was approximately 35 miles west of Jordan in Garfield County.  The fires threatened 50 residences and 150 outbuildings.  The complex was difficult to contain due to the terrain in coulees and tree filled gorges that made fire fighting and fire suppression both dangerous and difficult.  The fire made it's largest run on the 19th due to a combination of high temperatures and low relative humidity. The Curry Coulee RAWS site had a high temperature of 103F and relative humidity of 7 percent on the afternoon of the 19th.  In fact, the temperature at the Curry Coulee raws site jumped to 113F during the afternoon of the 19th as the fire passed over the recording equipment. The weather was unusually hot and dry from the 16th through the 24th with daily highs in the upper 90s to around 105F and relative humidity values frequently dropped into the single digits during the afternoon.  In addition to the heat and dry conditions, strong winds helped to rapidly spread the fire on the 19th when wind gust of 40 to 45 mph were reported during the afternoon. Relative humidity recovery was poor at night with relative humidity values remained between 25 and 30 percent. The fire were declared under control at 5:50 pm on July 27th after burning 130,927 acres. A total of 8 structures were destoyed and one was damaged. The fire destroyed 610 miles of barb wired fence . This was the largest fire at that point and time in the United States.

A strong cold front crossed northeast Montana during the morning hours of the 17th.  Strong gradient winds occurred immediately behind the front.  Sustained wind speeds of 40 to 50 mph were common with wind gusts of up to 68 mph.  The highest measured wind gust was 68 mph at King Coulee Raws in northern Garfield county.  A semi truck and trailer was blown off Interstate 94 in Dawson County, and overturned while attempting to exit the highway.  The driver suffered minor injuries.

Lightning ignited numerous small wildfires across Garfield, Petroleum, and Southern Phillips Counties. While most of the fires were 100 acres or less, the Blue Pulaski fire reached 3000 acres near Hell Creek Recreation area north of Jordan before it was contained.

A major winter storm, which affected all of northeast Montana, and produced blizzard conditions at times, began during the morning hours on December 26th and continued through the morning hours of the 28th.  The storm produced up to 30 inches of snowfall, and set many snowfall records.  Low pressure developed in Wyoming on the morning of the 26th.  The low gradually intensified as it tracked into the Dakotas on the 27th.  Snow developed across Petroleum, Garfield, and Phillips counties during the morning hours of the 26th, and spread into Valley County during the afternoon. The snow overspread the remainder of northeast Montana during the evening and overnight hours of the 26th. At the onset of the storm, there was a period of freezing rain which produced up to a quarter of an inch of ice across portions of Prairie, Wibaux, Dawson, Richland, Roosevelt and McCone Counties.  Snow fell heavily at times during the night, and continued through the 27th.  North winds of 20 to 40 mph created blizzard conditions at times across most of northeast Montana. The snow and wind left most highways either closed or only open to emergency travel.  Highway 191 in Phillips County from Malta to Grass Range was closed through December 30th with snow drifts up to 10 feet deep, and there were 50 vehicles stuck on this stretch of road from Malta to Zortman.  Snow drifts of 5 to 6 feet were reported in Zortman. The hardest hit areas were Phillips, Northern Petroleum, Western Valley, and Western Garfield Counties were 18 to 30 inches of snow fell.  Lesser amounts of snow fell further east across Sheridan, eastern Roosevelt, Richland, southern Dawson, Wibaux and portions of Prairie County where most snowfall totals were less than 8 inches.  Although snow amounts were less near the North Dakota border, the wind was stronger with sustained wind speeds of 30 to 45 mph and gusts to 60 mph.  The blizzard conditions continued through the morning hours of the 28th along the North Dakota border where the wind remained strong.  Some snowfall totals across northeast Montana included: Glasgow: 13.7 inchesNashua: 12 inchesHinsdale: 18 inchesFort Peck: 15 inchesZortman: 30 inchesMalta: 18 inchesPort of Morgan: 12 inchesBrusett: 24 inchesFlatwillow: 14 inchesJordan: 12 inchesMedicine Lake: 10 inches

Lightning ignited a series of wildfires starting on July 14. The Mickey Butte fire in Southwest Valley and Southeast Phillips Counties reached 3500 acres before it was contained on July 20. The wildfires in Northwest Garfield County eventually became the Missouri Breaks Complex which is covered in more detail in a separate event. The remaining wildfires were 100 acres or less.

A major winter storm, which affected all of northeast Montana, and produced blizzard conditions at times, began during the morning hours on December 26th and continued through the morning hours of the 28th.  The storm produced up to 30 inches of snowfall, and set many snowfall records.  Low pressure developed in Wyoming on the morning of the 26th.  The low gradually intensified as it tracked into the Dakotas on the 27th.  Snow developed across Petroleum, Garfield, and Phillips counties during the morning hours of the 26th, and spread into Valley County during the afternoon. The snow overspread the remainder of northeast Montana during the evening and overnight hours of the 26th. At the onset of the storm, there was a period of freezing rain which produced up to a quarter of an inch of ice across portions of Prairie, Wibaux, Dawson, Richland, Roosevelt and McCone Counties.  Snow fell heavily at times during the night, and continued through the 27th.  North winds of 20 to 40 mph created blizzard conditions at times across most of northeast Montana. The snow and wind left most highways either closed or only open to emergency travel.  Highway 191 in Phillips County from Malta to Grass Range was closed through December 30th with snow drifts up to 10 feet deep, and there were 50 vehicles stuck on this stretch of road from Malta to Zortman.  Snow drifts of 5 to 6 feet were reported in Zortman. The hardest hit areas were Phillips, Northern Petroleum, Western Valley, and Western Garfield Counties were 18 to 30 inches of snow fell.  Lesser amounts of snow fell further east across Sheridan, eastern Roosevelt, Richland, southern Dawson, Wibaux and portions of Prairie County where most snowfall totals were less than 8 inches.  Although snow amounts were less near the North Dakota border, the wind was stronger with sustained wind speeds of 30 to 45 mph and gusts to 60 mph.  The blizzard conditions continued through the morning hours of the 28th along the North Dakota border where the wind remained strong.  Some snowfall totals across northeast Montana included: Glasgow: 13.7 inchesNashua: 12 inchesHinsdale: 18 inchesFort Peck: 15 inchesZortman: 30 inchesMalta: 18 inchesPort of Morgan: 12 inchesBrusett: 24 inchesFlatwillow: 14 inchesJordan: 12 inchesMedicine Lake: 10 inches

A semi truck over turned on I-94 approximately 10 miles southwest of Glendive.  There was one injury reported with broken bones.   Widespread wind and hail damage was reported in southern Dawson County.  A tree fell through a house and caused approximately 100 thousand dollars of damage.  Three grain bins were destroyed causing approximately 180 thousand dollars of damage.  There was approximately 120 thousand dollars of damage to beet crops.  Estimated total damage from the storm was 500 thousand dollars.

A lightning strike in the town of Whitetail caused a partial power outage in the town of Whitetail which destroyed two small transformers.

A high wind event occurred over a large portion of North Central Montana during daylight hours of the 18th.  Wind gusts from 90 to 100 mph were recorded along the Northern Rocky Mountain front. Power outages were reported throughout the area as well as minor  damage to various structures. A semi tractor-trailer was blown off of Highway 200 and the eastbound Amtrak passenger train was delayed for several hours due to the high winds.Peak wind speeds reported include: 100 mph at Browning,  94 mph at the Choteau Airport,  78 mph at Choteau 2E, 72 mph at Babb 25NW, 64 mph at the NWS office in Great Falls, 63 mph at MacDonald Pass, 62 mph at Del Bonita, and 60 mph at Sweetgrass.

High winds ahead of a strong cold front spread across western Montana during the morning hours of 11/18/03. Strongest wind recorded at the lower elevations was at Angel Point on Flathead Lake, with sustained southwest winds of 42 MPH gusting to 59 MPH. Over the mountain passes, McDonald Pass recorded sustained southwest winds of 50 MPH gusting to 61 MPH, and at Logan Pass wind gusts to 74 MPH. Montana Dept. of Transportation reported high winds around the southwest side Flathead Lake and over the higher elevations in the Butte/Pintlar Region. Northwest Montana lost power in many locations from downed trees onto power lines. Wind damaged a marina at Big Arm, and some carports in Ronan. In Polson, an 80-year-old Douglas fire tree was uprooted and toppled over.
Heavy snow fell over portions of northwest Montana. Snowfall totals ranged from 6 to 10 inches. Noxon reported the highest amount with 10 inches.

A line of thunderstorms moved across west central Montana during the evening hours, producing widespread outflow winds of 45 to 50 mph, with localized damaging winds of 60 to 70 mph. In Missoula County, thunderstorm winds blew down trees onto power lines and onto two vehicles causing power outages and damage to the vehicles. Near the confluence of Rock Creek and the Clark Fork River, thunderstorm winds of 71 mph caused roof damage and blew down trees. South of Seeley Lake, at the Boles Fire Camp, winds blew down tents. Near Dixon in Sanders County, thunderstorm winds of 59 mph caused roof damage to a trailer home. Power outages were reported in Ravalli County near Hamilton with trees blown down. In Mineral County, 50 mph winds caused the Fish Creek Fire to spread an additional 7000 acres, causing evacuation of homes in the Petty Creek area.
A wind gust of 58 mph occurred at the Deep Creek RAWS site.

Winter storm brought localized heavy snow to the valleys of northwest Montana, with strong gusty south winds and widespread heavy snow to the western Montana mountains. Snowfall totals ranged from 6 to 8 inches in the northwest Montana valleys, 8 to 14 inches at the ski resorts, and up to two feet over the higher terrain Snotel sites. Highest values recorded were 17 inches at Stahl Peak, 18 inches at Lookout  Pass, and 24 inches at Moss Peak and Noisy Basin. South winds of 15 to 30 MPH in the mountains caused areas of blowing and drifting of snow.

Strong winter storm brought heavy snow and strong winds to western Montana for a two day period. Cool front passed through western Montana during the afternoon and evening hours with gusty west winds behind the front. A dust devil formed over the Nine Mile fire camp with wind speeds estimated at 40 to 50 mph, which blew over a supply tent. Three men in the camp sustained minor injuries. Northwest sustained winds of 15 to 30 mph, with gusts to 36 mph over the Missoula area, fueled the Black Mountain Fire west of Missoula. The fire spread over 4000 acres during the evening hours forcing evacuations of more than 130 homes. Two homes were completely burned and lost on Cedar Creek Road.

The combination of hot and dry conditions along with dry lightning and wind events, led to widespread wildfires in western Montana. 750,000 acres burned in western Montana forests in August with 46 structures lost. The Wedge Fire in northern Flathead County, burned 35 homes, while the Black Mountain 2 fire west of Missoula, burned 5 homes.

Scattered strong thunderstorms moved through west central Montana during the late afternoon and early evening hours, causing widespread damage from severe winds. In Missoula County, wind gusts to 58 mph blew down large tree branches onto power lines in  Missoula and near Frenchtown. In the Seeley Lake area, over two dozen trees 12 inches in diameter, were blown down onto power lines, that also started fires. Wind gusts 60 to 64 mph were reported in Lake and Powell Counties, with thunderstorm winds blowing apart a shed and damaging a tin roof in the Ovando area. Tree damage and fire starts were reported up the Jocko River drainage. In Mineral County, 3/8 inch hail and wind gusts to 64 mph were reported near Deborgia.

A line of thunderstorms moved across west central Montana during the evening hours, producing widespread outflow winds of 45 to 50 mph, with localized damaging winds of 60 to 70 mph. In Missoula County, thunderstorm winds blew down trees onto power lines and onto two vehicles causing power outages and damage to the vehicles. Near the confluence of Rock Creek and the Clark Fork River, thunderstorm winds of 71 mph caused roof damage and blew down trees. South of Seeley Lake, at the Boles Fire Camp, winds blew down tents. Near Dixon in Sanders County, thunderstorm winds of 59 mph caused roof damage to a trailer home. Power outages were reported in Ravalli County near Hamilton with trees blown down. In Mineral County, 50 mph winds caused the Fish Creek Fire to spread an additional 7000 acres, causing evacuation of homes in the Petty Creek area.

Winter storm brought localized heavy snow to the valleys of northwest Montana, with strong gusty south winds and widespread heavy snow to the western Montana mountains. Snowfall totals ranged from 6 to 8 inches in the northwest Montana valleys, 8 to 14 inches at the ski resorts, and up to two feet over the higher terrain Snotel sites. Highest values recorded were 17 inches at Stahl Peak, 18 inches at Lookout  Pass, and 24 inches at Moss Peak and Noisy Basin. South winds of 15 to 30 MPH in the mountains caused areas of blowing and drifting of snow.

Localized heavy snow fell over the Missoula and Bitterroot Valleys, areas of the Bitterroot and Sapphire Mountains. Snowfall totals ranged from 4 to 7 inches in most areas. Highest amounts of new snow recorded were 8 inches at Lolo Hot Springs and 12 inches west of Conner at elevation 4217 feet.

The combination of hot and dry conditions along with dry lightning and wind events, led to widespread wildfires in western Montana. 750,000 acres burned in western Montana forests in August with 46 structures lost. The Wedge Fire in northern Flathead County, burned 35 homes, while the Black Mountain 2 fire west of Missoula, burned 5 homes.

A lightning caused wildfire started north of West Yellowstone on the 20th of August. This wildfire was named the Rathbone Fire. 2000 acres burned by the end of August, and an additional 1000 acres burned in September. No structural losses or injuries were reported. Traffic was limited to the day time hours only on Highway 191 near the fire due to the thick smoke.

A dust devil went through the Rathbone Wildfire Base Camp on the 3rd. The dust devil was on the ground for 1 minute, it was 6 feet wide, and traveled one half mile. The dust devil struck a few camping tents and one equipment tent. A few personal items and a laptop computer were damaged. Some of this debris was blown in the air about 3000 feet.

An arctic cold front moved across the Continental Divide bringing heavy snow and gusty winds to the region. Emergency travel was reported by the Montana Department of Transportation Tuesday night and early Wednesday morning across sections of the Mission and Flathead Valleys as well as areas near the Divide while the front moved through. Snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 8 inches at Alberton and St. Ignatius southward across Missoula, Lolo, and Darby. Heavier snow of 10 to 12 inches was reported at Lolo Hot Springs, Warm Springs, and Peterson Meadows. Gusty northeast winds of 20 to 40 mph were common during the event. Peak gusts included, 38 mph at Kalispell, 36 mph at Columbia Falls, and 34 mph at Thompson Falls.

The winds began to diminish in strength during the late evening hours of the 26th. Most areas reported wind gusts near 60 mph, with sustained winds between 35 and 45 mph. Here is a list of some of the peak wind gusts reported:East Glacier 2E 66 mph at 1240 MST; Choteau 62 mph at 1445 MST; Sweetgrass 60 mph at 1906 MST; Cut Bank 59 mph at 1538 MST; Pendroy 59 mph at 1820 MST; and Loma had sustained winds of 44 mph at 1315 MST. Gold Butte 7N 61 mph at 0055 MST on 1/27/03.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front combined with a strong upper level disturbance brought high winds to parts of northeast Montana. The peak wind speed was 73 mph at Whitewater.  Other high wind reports included 19 miles northwest of Vida 70 mph, King Coulee and Glasgow 67 mph, Bluff Creek RAWS 63 mph, Glendive 61 mph, Manning Corral RAWS 60 mph, South Sawmill RAWS and Big Sheep RAWS 59 mph, and Malta 58 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: 59 mph wind gust at South Sawmill RAWS.

A cold front moved south through central Montana during the morning hours on the 29th. Sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts near 60 mph were common. Falling snow reduced visibilities to one-half mile or less for several hours. Emergency travel only was advised over portions of North-Central Montana during the height of the storm.Here is a list of some snow fall amounts: Shonkin 7S 29 inches; Cleveland 15 inches; Lewistown 18SW 11 inches; Choteau 29W 10 inches;  Rocky Boy 10 inches; Dupuyer 25W 9 inches; Hobson 9 inches; Neihart 8NE 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell over the Rocky Mountain Front during the early morning hours of the 1st.  The snow let up in intensity over the northern portion of the Rocky Mountain Front during the mid morning hours, and then came to an end over Southwest Montana during the late afternoon hours.Here is a list of some of the snowfall totals received:12 inches at Choteau 30W; 8 inches at Lincoln 10N; 6 to 8 inches at St. Mary's 10W (SNOTEL); 6 inches at Alder 20SE (SNOTEL) and West Yellowstone 15NW (SNOTEL).

After 5 days of temperatures near 40 degrees, an arctic cold front pushed south through Montana on the 22nd. Skies were cloudy across the region, along with areas of snow on the 23rd. By late evening on the 23rd, skies cleared, and temperatures began to fall quickly. By the mid morning hours on the 24th the air temperatures over Southwest Montana ranged from -40  F to nearly -50  F, while temperatures ranged from -30  F to nearly -40  F over North Central Montana.Here is a list of some of the morning low temperatures received:-49  F at Wisdom; -45  F at West Yellowstone; -38  F at Chinook; -35  F at Belgrade; and -31  F at Havre. All of these temperatures were new daily record lows for each of these cities.Additionally, the -49  F at Wisdom was the 4th coldest temperature ever for the month of February, and the 12th coldest temperature of all time.In West Yellowstone, the -45  F was the 15th coldest ever for the month of February.

With a major snowstorm falling in the mountains in the Helena Valley on March 6th through the 10th, and temperatures quickly rising to near 70 degrees by the 12th, a rapid snow melt occurred in the mountains. This snow melt quickly filled all small streams and creeks. As the snow continued to melt during the afternoon hours, the creeks began to overflow, as some culverts were blocked with ice and debris. The result was some nearby homes and roads next to the creeks were either damaged or washed out. Water was up to a foot deep in some locations. Damage reports in the Helena Valley included about 20 homes with flood damage, and a few secondary roads washed out. The damage cost was estimated at $250,000. In the Bozeman area, a few gravel roads were washed out, along with a small bridge. Damage costs were estimated at $500,000. Further north, flooding did occur on small creeks flowing out of the Bears Paw Mountains south of Havre. No damage was reported, but a few rural roads were under 6 inches of water during the afternoon hours on the 12th and 13th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: 16 inches of snow at the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Billings

Strong winter storm impacted most of western Montana for several days with heavy snow and gusty north to northwest winds. Storm total snowfall ranged from 5 to 7 inches in the west central Montana valleys, to over a foot in the mountains. The Bitterroot Mountains and southwest Montana received the most amount of snow with Anaconda receiving 15 inches, Lolo Pass 19 inches, Twin Lakes 20 inches, and Nez Perce Camp 23 inches. Northerly winds of 15 to 30 mph with localized gusts of 35 to 45 mph, caused areas of blowing and drifting of snow and poor visibility. At the Missoula Airport, strong winds blew over a 50 foot Poplar tree.

Heavy snow fell over the entire Rocky Mountain Front from March 6th to March 10th. Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches per day. However, in a 24 hour period starting at 0400 MST on the 7th, some areas did receive over 2 feet of snow. By the end of the storm, many locations above 5000 feet had received between 3 and 5 feet of snow.The amount of the snowfall over this 5 day period caused a car port to collapse, crushing a vehicle underneath it.Here is a list of some of the snowfalls reported for the total storm:56 inches at East Glacier; 48 inches at Landers Park; 37 inches at Lincoln 6N (SNOTEL); 36 inches at Lincoln; 32 inches at Many Glacier; 26 inches at Pike Creek; and 21 inches at Badger Pass.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: 6 inches of snow with 4 foot drifts 10 miles north of Roundup

Blowing and drifting of the snow was widespread, as winds were sustained near 30 mph, with gusts to 40 mph.Here is a list of some of the snowfalls reported:10 inches at Neihart 10S, Shonkin 7S, and White Sulphur Springs 40N; 9 inches at Lewistown 22S, and Clancy 10SE; and 3 to 5 inches throughout the Rocky Mountain Front.

A blizzard occurred over the entire Rocky Mountain Front on March 5th. Sustained winds near 35 mph, along with heavy snow fell from East Glacier south to Lincoln. Significant blowing and drifting of the snow occurred. One to two feet of snowfall was common over the Rocky Mountain Front in a 24 hour period ending by the early morning hours on the 6th. Here is a list of some of the snowfalls reported:24 inches at Babb 10W and East Glacier Park 10SW; 18 inches at Lincoln 6N; 14 inches at Lincoln; 13 inches at Bynum 24W; and 12 inches at Pendroy 20W and St. Mary.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winter storm impacted most of western Montana for several days with heavy snow and gusty north to northwest winds. Storm total snowfall ranged from 5 to 7 inches in the west central Montana valleys, to over a foot in the mountains. The Bitterroot Mountains and southwest Montana received the most amount of snow with Anaconda receiving 15 inches, Lolo Pass 19 inches, Twin Lakes 20 inches, and Nez Perce Camp 23 inches. Northerly winds of 15 to 30 mph with localized gusts of 35 to 45 mph, caused areas of blowing and drifting of snow and poor visibility. At the Missoula Airport, strong winds blew over a 50 foot Poplar tree.

A long duration winter storm impacted a large portion of western Montana from March 5 through March 8. Pacific storm systems marched inland during the week as an Arctic cold front waffled back and forth along the Continental Divide, resulting in moist air overrunning cold air, and bouts of gusty winds that created poor visibilities and blowing or drifting snow. The Department of Transportation declared emergency travel for different highways due to poor driving conditions during each day of the event. Storm totals during the 4 days ranged from many inches in valley locations to several feet in the mountains; 10-15 inches in the Kootenai Region, 1-3 feet West Glacier Region with the exception of 52 inches, or more than 4 feet, at Marias Pass, 1-2 feet Lower Clark Fork Region, 2-4 feet at higher elevations of the Bitterroot and Pintlar Mountains and the Blackfoot Region, up to 16 inches in the Bitterroot Valley.

A line of thunderstorms with wind gusts exceeding 60 mph, moved through Sanders, Lake and Flathead Counties, leaving a wake of wind damage to power lines, trees, buildings and airplanes. As the storms moved through Sanders County, strong winds knocked down trees onto power lines and injured a man in Paradise. In the town of Dixon, strong winds blew roofs off homes that hit and damaged the Old Dixon Store. As the storms moved through Lake County, wind damage was reported from Ronan to the southern end of Flathead Lake. Strong winds at the Ronan Airport caused the ropes to break off a Cessna 150, which careened into a Cessna 172, damaging both airplanes. Trees were reported uprooted near Finley Point and Polson, leaving at least two houses damaged from fallen trees. Strong wind gusts tore off part of the face of the Old Mill Place along Main Street in Polson, damaging a vehicle. 10 miles west of Polson, a small barn was blown over in Irvine Flats. Before the storms weakened in Flathead County, a twelve inchdiameter tree was blown over near Big Fork, damaging a home owners fence.

Heavy snow and cold temperatures from the week before, followed by temperatures in the 50s and gusty southwest winds lead to a large avalanche along Highway 2 between Essex and Marias Pass. The main slide occurred north of Silver Stair Falls snapping a highway guardrail and blocking both lanes of traffic. An automobile reportedly ran into the snow slide, but no injuries or property damage were reported. The Montana DOT closed the highway for two days due to the high potential for additional avalanches.
Heavy snow fell overnight in the West Glacier Region with DOT reports of 6-11 inches along Highway 2 between Coram and Marias Pass.

With a major snowstorm falling in the mountains in the Helena Valley on March 6th through the 10th, and temperatures quickly rising to near 70 degrees by the 12th, a rapid snow melt occurred in the mountains. This snow melt quickly filled all small streams and creeks. As the snow continued to melt during the afternoon hours, the creeks began to overflow, as some culverts were blocked with ice and debris. The result was some nearby homes and roads next to the creeks were either damaged or washed out. Water was up to a foot deep in some locations. Damage reports in the Helena Valley included about 20 homes with flood damage, and a few secondary roads washed out. The damage cost was estimated at $250,000. In the Bozeman area, a few gravel roads were washed out, along with a small bridge. Damage costs were estimated at $500,000. Further north, flooding did occur on small creeks flowing out of the Bears Paw Mountains south of Havre. No damage was reported, but a few rural roads were under 6 inches of water during the afternoon hours on the 12th and 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep snow pack from the previous weeks winter storms and a day of high temperatures in the 60s, lead to extensive snow melt and several small streams overflowing their banks in the Deer Lodge area. Two homes were damaged by flood waters getting into basements. City crews sandbagged to protect homes and two residents were evacuated during the flood event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration winter storm impacted a large portion of western Montana from March 5 through March 8. Pacific storm systems marched inland during the week as an Arctic cold front waffled back and forth along the Continental Divide, resulting in moist air overrunning cold air, and bouts of gusty winds that created poor visibilities and blowing or drifting snow. The Department of Transportation declared emergency travel for different highways due to poor driving conditions during each day of the event. Storm totals during the 4 days ranged from many inches in valley locations to several feet in the mountains; 10-15 inches in the Kootenai Region, 1-3 feet West Glacier Region with the exception of 52 inches, or more than 4 feet, at Marias Pass, 1-2 feet Lower Clark Fork Region, 2-4 feet at higher elevations of the Bitterroot and Pintlar Mountains and the Blackfoot Region, up to 16 inches in the Bitterroot Valley.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowmobiler was tragically killed in an avalanche on the flanks of Mount Abundance in the mountains north of Cooke City. The slide path was small, only 200 feet wide by 250 feet vertical, yet it broke 2-3 feet deep on the ice crust and facets. A rider got stuck and then two friends rode up to help him, which triggered the slide. It was triggered on a slope 30-33 degrees, but the terrain steepened above them with an average angle of 37 degrees before finally reaching into the low 40s near the windloaded ridgetop. Two people were buried, but one was able to clear snow from his face. A beacon search found the victim within 15 minutes, and even though his head was only two feet from the surface, his friends were unable to revive him.
14 inches of snow at Red Lodge Mountain

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms with wind gusts exceeding 60 mph, moved through Sanders, Lake and Flathead Counties, leaving a wake of wind damage to power lines, trees, buildings and airplanes. As the storms moved through Sanders County, strong winds knocked down trees onto power lines and injured a man in Paradise. In the town of Dixon, strong winds blew roofs off homes that hit and damaged the Old Dixon Store. As the storms moved through Lake County, wind damage was reported from Ronan to the southern end of Flathead Lake. Strong winds at the Ronan Airport caused the ropes to break off a Cessna 150, which careened into a Cessna 172, damaging both airplanes. Trees were reported uprooted near Finley Point and Polson, leaving at least two houses damaged from fallen trees. Strong wind gusts tore off part of the face of the Old Mill Place along Main Street in Polson, damaging a vehicle. 10 miles west of Polson, a small barn was blown over in Irvine Flats. Before the storms weakened in Flathead County, a twelve inchdiameter tree was blown over near Big Fork, damaging a home owners fence.

Ice jams on the Yellowstone River between March 16th-18th caused flooding of low lying areas. The flooding caused some minor damage in Prairie and Dawson Counties.  In addition to the ice jams, heavy rain partially contributed to the flooding of several fields and roads, and later washed out a gravel road in Richland County.  On the 17th, 2.00 inches of rain fell at Crane, 1.79 inches of rain fell at Sidney and 1.71 inches of rain fell at Savage. The Yellowstone River at Glendive crested at 60.5 feet at 10 pm on March 16th.  The flood stage at Glendive is 53.5 feet.  The water receded after the ice jam broke on the afternoon of March 18.

Due to very warm temperatures over much of Southwest Montana during the last week of May, a significant snow melt began to occur at higher elevations in the mountains of Southwest Montana. By May 30th, the Big Hole, Gallatin, and the Jefferson Rivers all began to overflow their banks. The rivers continued to rise, cresting during the first week of June.

Snow accumulations occurred over all of the Plains, but was heaviest in the mountains.Here is a list of some of the snowfalls reported:8 inches at Benchland; 7 inches in Neihart 8N, Carter 27NW, Lewistown 22S, and Utica 20SW; 6 inches at Moore, 5 inches at Hobson, and 4 inches in Great Falls and East Glacier Park.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: 1.00 inch hail covering ground

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms moved through Ravalli and Missoula Counties between 1250 PM and 240 PM MST, producing large hail and damaging winds. In southern Ravalli County near Sula and Conner, thunderstorms produced three quarter to one inch diameter hail and large branches were blown off a willow tree. Northwest of Missoula near the Wye, a severe thunderstorm produced golf ball size hail, which damaged the Kinderhaus Montessori School's pizza garden. As the storm traveled north over Evaro Hill, it blew a roof off a home which also damaged a parked vehicle. Crop damage was also reported from hail. A Pattee Canyon house was severely damaged from a lightning caused fire. Near Seeley Lake, strong thunderstorm winds blew down trees, which fell onto a home.

A severe thunderstorm moved northeast across Central Cascade County during the evening hours of the 19th. One to two inches of rain fell in 30 minutes as the storm moved through. Flash flooding did occur in the towns of Sand Coulee and Tracy. Water flooded 25 homes and left roadways filled with mud and debris.

A 46 year old man was struck by lightning on a motorcycle on Highway 200 approximately 10 miles west of Sydney. The man was burned on his left shoulder. The lightning exited at the ball of his right foot. He was treated at the hospital and released the next day.

The high temperature reached 100 degrees or higher for 6 consecutive days at Glasgow International Airport from August 11th-16th.  This breaks the previous record of 5 consecutive days with highs of 100 degrees or higher set back in 1933.  In addition, several record high minimum temperatures were recorded on the 12th through the 17th.  The excessive heat was widespread across Northeast Montana with many cooperative observers reporting 6 consecutive days with high temperatures of 100 or higher.  The highest temperature recorded during the record breaking heatwave was 106 which was recorded on the 16th at Downtown Glasgow and 4 miles southwest of Hinsdale, as well as Glendive on the 13th.

The high temperature reached 100 degrees or higher for 6 consecutive days at Glasgow International Airport from August 11th-16th.  This breaks the previous record of 5 consecutive days with highs of 100 degrees or higher set back in 1933.  In addition, several record high minimum temperatures were recorded on the 12th through the 17th.  The excessive heat was widespread across Northeast Montana with many cooperative observers reporting 6 consecutive days with high temperatures of 100 or higher.  The highest temperature recorded during the record breaking heatwave was 106 which was recorded on the 16th at Downtown Glasgow and 4 miles southwest of Hinsdale, as well as Glendive on the 13th.

Lightning caused approximately 10K in damage to the phone system at City Hall. There was also approximately 1K in damage to the ceiling in the fire hall as a result of a lightning strike.
A lightning strike to an old cottonwood tree started a fire that burned approximately 60 acres of pasture and an old land fill.
There were several small wildfires started by lightning throughout the county.  The fires burned approximately 600 acres.
There were several small wildfires in Prarie County.  Approximately 1000 acres burned.
A wildfire believed to have been started by a combine striking a rock during dry conditions burned approximately 7000 acres.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dust Devil picked up tethered portable inflatable slide and  injured 4 people who required hospitalization.

Damage survey determined tornado destroyed 1 mobile home and badly damage another. Several steel grain bins destroyed by straight line winds along with large cottonwood trees snapped in half along with trees having leaves stripped off. Mobile home was not occupied at the time.

A powerful pacific storm with a very strong jet stream brought strong gusty winds to western Montana. Wind gusts to 60 mph blewdown trees and caused power outages near Whitefish in northwest Montana and in Anaconda in southwest Montana. A 50 foot evergreen tree and metal storage shed in Anaconda were blown down causing minor damage. Highest wind gust recorded was64 mph at Deer Mountain in the Bitterroot Mountains.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong gradient winds affected a portion of Northeast Montana. 62 mph wind gusts were reported at the King Coulee Raws. 58 mph wind gusts were reported 5 miles southeast of Plentywood. 59 mph wind gusts were reported in Plentywood.

The first winter storm of the season affected northern Valley County and the Little Rockies. 6 inches of heavy wet snow fell at Zortman, and 5 miles south of Opheim. The Opheim area also had drifts up to 5 feet high caused by winds up to 40 mph.

A severe thunderstorm moved across the southern sections Great Falls between 1945 and 2000 MST. Hail up to 0.75 inches and wind gusts to 58 mph occurred as the storm moved through. A funnel cloud was reported over the southern sections of Great Falls between 1950 and 2000 MST. Damage was minor, with several power lines were knocked down, and some minor street flooding.
A severe thunderstorm produced 1.00 inch hail in Chester.
A severe thunderstorm produced a 68 mph wind gust at Great Falls 3SW.
A severe thunderstorm produced a 60 mph wind gust in Great Falls.

A youth group of kids and a few adults were rafting down the Missouri River during the late afternoon hours on the 13th. A fast moving thunderstorm approached and a lightning bolt struck the raft. Most of the people in the raft fell into the water. Four children suffered minor burns. One adult was struck directly by the lightning bolt and needed CPR to be resuscitated. Serious bruising and trauma also resulted.
A wind gust of 73 mph occurred at Chinook 11S.
A wind gust of 71 mph occurred at Chinook 11S.
A wind gust of 62 mph occurred at Chinook 11S.

A lightning caused wildfire started north of West Yellowstone on the 20th of August. This wildfire was named the Rathbone Fire. 2000 acres burned by the end of August. No structural losses or injuries were reported. Traffic was limited to the day time hours only on Highway 191 near the fire due to the thick smoke.
